entry.content:
Introduction

This article discusses reverse engineering an eCos RTOS embedded system.

Firmware Extraction


The SoC(System-on-Chip), SPI SOP-8 flash ROM chip, UART(Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter) pin and Wireless LAN chip were on the PCB. So I just chipped off the flash ROM chip for the firmware extraction.


The flash ROM was a BoHong BH25D16A chip, and the pin map was as shown below:

  • 1: CS(Chip Select)
  • 2: DO(Serial Data Output)
  • 3: WP(Write protect when driven LOW)
  • 4: VSS(Ground)
  • 5: DI(Serial Data Input)
  • 6: SCK(Serial Clock)
  • 7: NC(Not Connection)
  • 8: VCC(Supply Voltage)

The BH25D16A is designed for environments where it has dedicated (non-shared) access to the SPI bus, so there is no HOLD pin for yielding the bus to other devices. Because I only wanted to read the entire memory, I left the WP pin floating (not needed for read-only operations).

SPI

Before extracting the firmware, I had to understand how SPI communication works. SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) is a synchronous serial communication protocol in which one master communicates with one or more slave devices in full-duplex. In this case, the master is the SPI programmer and the slave is the BH25D16A.

PinDirectionDescription
MOSI(Master Out Slave In)Master → SlaveMaster’s data send line
MISO(Master In Slave Out)Slave → MasterSlave’s data send line
SCLK(Serial Clock)Master → SlaveMaster’s clock signal
CS/SS(Chip Select / Slave Select)Master → SlaveSelects slave with driven LOW

Clock Signal

The clock signal plays a role like beats that say “Read the data now” and “Send it now”, because multiple devices must work at the same time. So the master notifies the slave to sample data at the clock edge (rising edge in SPI Mode 0/3).

SPI Communication Diagram

The BH25D16A’s maximum clock frequency is 55 MHz for normal read and 108 MHz for fast/dual read. I used a CH341A programmer to communicate with the flash ROM via SPI. The flashrom‘s spispeed was fixed at 2 MHz, so I could stably read the ROM data without modifying it.



Finally, I got the RTOS firmware! To verify the stability of the binary, I extracted twice and compared the MD5 hash of each binary.

Booting Sequence

Before starting to analyze the firmware, I had to identify the SoC memory map to create segments with correct addresses.

So first, I removed the EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) shield to check the SoC label.

Boot Stage1

The SoC was RTL8197F series.


According to the datasheet, the bootloader code starts in image base 0xB0000000 via XIP(eXecute In Place).

The bootloader first prints the “Booting…” log message, invalidates the D-cache (Data cache) and I-cache (Instruction cache), copies the Stage1.5 Bootloader (0xB0007000) to DRAM (0x80100000), and jumps to it.
So I created a new DRAM segment like below:

Boot Stage1.5

The Stage1.5 Bootloader decompresses the gzip compressed Stage2 Interactive Bootloader (0x80101D60) to KSEG0 (0x80000000), synchronizes the code cache (D-cache writeback + I-cache invalidate), and runs it.

Boot Stage2

The Stage2 Interactive Bootloader checks the firmware’s integrity.

Firmware Integrity Check

The check_firmware_integrity function checks firmware magic bytes against cs6c and cr6c, which are Realtek firmware magic bytes.

  • cs6c: LZMA compressed firmware image
  • cr6c: root filesystem image

Because this is an RTOS firmware, I could see the cs6c magic bytes (no filesystem) at offset 0x20000 of the firmware. Additionally, it verifies the rootfs checksum if the magic is cr6c.

After the integrity check, it loads the Kernel Decompressor to KSEG0 (0x80700000) and runs it.

Kernel Decompressor

The LZMA (Lempel-Ziv-Markov chain Algorithm) Decompressor decompresses the kernel to KSEG1 (0xA0000000).

Why KSEG1 for Kernel Decompression Output?

The decompressed kernel is written to 0xA0000000 (KSEG1, uncached), not 0x80000000 (KSEG0, cached). Both map to the same physical DRAM address 0x00000000:

Writing to KSEG0 (0x80000000, cached) would place the decompressed data in D-cache, and eventually write it back to DRAM. However, since the MIPS architecture does not maintain coherency between D-cache and I-cache, the I-cache would still hold stale instructions — resulting in a crash.

By writing to KSEG1 (0xA0000000, uncached) instead, the decompressed data bypasses the cache and goes directly to DRAM. When the CPU later jumps to 0x80000600 (KSEG0), it triggers an I-cache miss and correctly fetches the fresh instructions from DRAM.

Therefore, when the decompression ends, the decompress_kernal_n_jump function runs the eCos kernel via jr 0xA0000600.

eCos Firmware

When first entering during boot, vector_table[0] is read because the ExcCode in the Cause register is 0(reset). Since the table is not set initially, in reality, the reset vector goes directly to 0x80000D00.

C++ Static Constructors: Thread Registration

The hal_reset_entry function(0x80000D00) initializes the exception vector table(0x80000400) and the SoC registers. And it calls cyg_hal_invoke_constructors function to register the main functionality thread.
cyg_hal_invoke_constructors iterates the .ctors section — a linker-generated array of function pointers. Each pointer is a C++ static constructor that runs before main

These constructors are generated by the eCos CYG_THREAD macro. Each one calls cyg_thread_create to register a thread with the scheduler:

Scheduler Dispatch: Indirect Call to xxx_app_main

After all constructors have run and threads are registered, cyg_scheduler_start enters the scheduling loop:

The scheduler loads the thread struct pointer from its run queue, reads the entry field at offset 0x14, and performs a context switch.

Full Booting Diagram
entry.title: Tearing Down eCos RTOS Embedded System

entry.content:
Introduction

This article discusses the restoration of corrupted embedded kernel using an SD card (Secure Digital card).

How I destroyed the kernel image

In my case, I attached an 8-pin SOIC clip directly to the NOR flash chip while the SoC was still on the board, which caused bus contention between the SoC and the SPI programmer. This likely resulted in unintended writes to the NOR flash, corrupting the kernel image and causing the boot sequence to fail during the checksum verification stage.
Using an 8-pin SOIC clip to restore the kernel image is not impossible, but I was just scared to use it again. So I found another way to restore the kernel image using an SD card.

To restore the corrupted kernel image, I should extract the kernel image from the original firmware and flash it into the kernel partition of the NOR flash memory. I used the original firmware, that I extracted before.
By the way, In case you can only download a legacy firmware online, I believe flashing an entire legacy firmware in NOR flash chip and updating it to a stable firmware using apps, after reviving the device, is the one of the firmware restoring method.

On the first attempt, I extracted the kernel image through binwalk from the original firmware and flashed it into the kernel partition of the NOR flash memory. However, the device still failed to boot it.
This is because binwalk extracts only the payload data (1,568,528 bytes) without the 64-byte uImage header. The uImage header contains critical metadata including the CRC checksum, load address, and entry point — without it, U-Boot cannot recognize the data as a valid kernel image.
Therefore, I had to extract 1,568,592 bytes (0x1E86A7 - 0x69757 = uImage header + kernel data) from the kernel image offset to include the complete uImage structure.

After extracting the kernel image, I had to flash it into the kernel partition.
By checking the mtdparts environment variable through the bootloader shell using printenv command, I could see that the linux partition, which contains the kernel image, starts at address 0x100000 and has a size of 0x1b0000.

Therefore, I had to flash the kernel image extracted earlier into the linux partition. In my case, I used the fatload command to load the kernel image into the device RAM, so I had to format the SD card as FAT32 in advance.

Formatting SD card

Since I was using macOS, I used the diskutil command to format the SD card. In my case, the SD card was /dev/disk4.

After formatting the SD card, I copied the kernel image I extracted earlier to the SD card.

Restoring the kernel image

Back to the bootloader shell, first I attached the SD card to the device, and then I checked if the device could recognize the SD card and set the current MMC (MultiMediaCard) device through the mmc command. In my case, the MMC index of the SD card was 0.

After setting the current MMC device, I loaded the kernel image, which I stored in the SD card, into 0x00000000 address of the device RAM through the fatload command.

On the second attempt, I tried to boot the device from 0x00000000 address of RAM through bootm command to see the result of the checksum verification. The checksum verification of the kernel image succeeded. However, as expected, the boot sequence still failed.

The checksum was valid, but bootm still failed because the kernel image at 0x00000000 overlaps with the load address 0x00008000 — the image spans 0x00000000–0x17EF90, and U-Boot needs to relocate it to 0x00008000, which falls within the source range. This overlap prevents U-Boot from safely copying the kernel to its load address.
To flash the NOR flash memory, I used the sf command and set the current SPI (NOR) flash chip. In my case, the index of the NOR flash chip was 0.

After setting the current NOR flash chip, I flashed kernel image, which is loaded on 0x00000000 address of the device RAM, into 0x100000 address of NOR flash memory, which is kernel partition, 0x1b0000 bytes.

After flashing kernel partition, finally I could boot the device through nvt_boot command. I could see the bootcmd environment variable through printenv command.
Additionally, you can also flash the entire firmware into the NOR flash memory through the same method. If the bootloader is also corrupted and cannot load anything, perform a chip-off extraction (optional) and use an 8-pin SOIC clip to flash the entire firmware into the NOR flash chip.

Device revived! :D
entry.summary:
Introduction

This article discusses the
entry.title: Embedded System Kernel Recovery
